speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two, Regina Weber. And it's the 10th of March, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 30 seconds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regina, we were talking about the Nuremberg laws and how they affected your life and your family's life. Can you give us a little more?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much more. I know that it affected the fact that my father lost his export license. So that part of the business was gone. We had land near Kisvarda. That was taken away. And we just couldn't go to higher schools. I-- otherwise I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother thought, or I thought, I'd better learn how to sew. I better learn something.

speaker: interviewer
text: A trade.

speaker: interviewee
text: A trade, which I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned, well, mainly everything about it, like cutting and sewing. And-- and I always designed everything for my dolls. I mean, they wore the same clothes as my mother did. I had access to beautiful fabrics, and I loved to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the rest of the girls-- were they also learning trades?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember. I guess so. But not all of them. You know, some--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother, Fischel?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did he do, Fischel? I-- I guess I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: March 19, 1944 was the day that Hitler marched into Budapest. How was that day in Kisvarda?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been just the end of the world, you know, because they started right away, the Nyilas party in Hungary, which really brought them in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us who the Nyilas are, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nyilas was the Hungarian fascist party. And they immediately started to speak against the Jews. And we're going to get the Jews. And the Jews are no good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear this?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard this on the radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard this on the radio. But you know, when you're a child, it doesn't sink in. You're still hopeful. You don't believe that that is real. You know, You don't want to believe that tragedy is going to happen to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother's at home with six children. Your father's in the United States. And you're hearing this on the radio. Do you remember how you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all felt very scared. And as I tell you, I was too young to believe it. And I had to eventually. [CHUCKLES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Tell us why you had to eventually believe it. You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because pretty soon they got us together. They just let us stay home for Passover, which my mother bought 300 eggs. Because she said, the day they took us away to the ghetto, she took 50 eggs. And I said, I am making a big omelette or something. This may be the last time I'm feeding my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: She knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember she said this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She knew it. She was very alert.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came to take you to the ghetto, and where was the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think anybody came. They just told us what to do. Take, I don't know, a few belongings, and no dishes, nothing, and that we had to go to one room was assigned to us on [HUNGARIAN]. And we just were already in a daze. And we all went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you? Was there a sign?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the Hungarian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gendarme was very much involved with this. And the Germans sent them first.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were posters or something that announced all-- do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that maybe. And-- but we knew. They came to your house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. The Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian gendarme. And you had to get yourself together, just how many pants you can take along, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: The gendarme were the police?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gendarme was the Hungarian police, which was, of course, part of that whole fascistic country.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were not Nyilas. They were not-- they were not Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, but they were anti-Semites. And they were glad to do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK. So do you remember them coming to your house? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you remember-- do you remember being in that one room with seven people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even there I have a lot of lost memory. But I remember something funny that my brother's friend says. You know, I'm in love with your little sister, Regina. And I said to him, no way. I don't care for him. [CHUCKLES] That human beings want to hold onto life, and some kind of normalcy, except in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- how-- the ghetto was in Kisvarda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was on one street.

speaker: interviewee
text: One street.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all these people were herded into one room.

speaker: interviewee
text: One room, seven people we were in.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think around 3,000 people were in this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there were Hungarian-- Kisvarda and vicinity, and I think there were 3,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a wall or a fence or anything around the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being able to go out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't go out much, no. We were afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Maybe my brother did because he wanted to be able to get some food or something. He became the father at his tender age. And I think he went out. But we didn't go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he-- how did you get food? Just through him, you think? You don't remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 17.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but going on 10, as I told you, because I was a very protected child.

speaker: interviewer
text: From a-- from a very orthodox family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox, very fine Jewish family, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where girls were very protected.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very protected.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the room in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Actually--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you slept? Was it on the floor, a bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have conception of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any Jewish police in the ghetto? Was there anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your mother's attitude in-- in-- in the-- in the ghetto? How was she-- how did she behave? Was she afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was just trying to hold us together. And she didn't show her fear because she loved us all, and she had to be strong. She said, while I have my children together, it's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was, you said, in May?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it lasted till May 28, when deportation started.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say deportation, what are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't tell us Auschwitz. They didn't tell us anything. Just, you're going to get on this train, which was a cattle train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody came to the apartment? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember that day. They had everybody's name, and they had everybody's family. And they had us line up with almost-- not even a suitcase.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarians, yeah. I think we didn't get to the Germans till we got to the train. And we just had very little things. And we had to carry it on our back. And we went, all the 3,000 people kind of marched through town. And the Hungarian peasants and everybody was lining the streets and laughing and saying things against the Jews. And by that time, we couldn't understand what we were doing. In a matter of less than two months, we became nonpeople. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the Hungarian Christians lining up on the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody came to give water, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. Nothing-- nothing-- nothing-- nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- how many guards were guarding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw-- you remember a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and they yell and scream, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No dogs-- I-- very few dogs. It was nothing like the German army, no. German SS, that is. But it was bad, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: These 3,000 Jews are marching down the street. Is it at night or during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the day. A beautiful day. May is a beautiful month. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they saying anything, the-- the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am sure they did, you know, just antisemitic remarks.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just-- just remember people standing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Laughing maybe, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Laughing. You remember laughing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You know, like-- I don't know what they said.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you remember marching.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you get to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think to the railroad station. And there already were the Nazis, the SS--

speaker: interviewer
text: German?

speaker: interviewee
text: --With the dogs, working with the Hungarians, the police. And they pushed us on this horrendous-- I don't know how many people in one car.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a regular train, like a subway?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was a-- a cattle car.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where they would keep cattle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there would be no seats. I think we had to sit on the floor. And we were all-- you know, all together with my mother. You know. And she just looked at us with those beautiful, sad eyes and repeating again, as long as I have my children, everything is all right. But it wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the ride from Kisvarda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you couldn't breathe. It took two days, or two nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were going from Kisvarda to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know where we were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's in actuality where--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were shipped to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Took two days. Do you remember anybody on the train and any reactions on-- on the cattle car, do you remember any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could tell you this one story that my brother got off, and somehow he wanted to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother escaped from the car?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No he didn't. And he heard my mother say, I have an only son, and he has left me. And he came back. He heard my mother say that. And he couldn't leave my mother. There was such love between-- you know, my only boy, you know, my mother's only son.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she must have not known where you were going.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she did. I think because she read-- we used to have Polish escapees in our house. And my mother took part in everything that had-- that the war brought to us. You know? And I think she knew it. But she wouldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had Polish escapees come to Kisvarda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then my mother took them to Budapest. And-- and we-- it was an unbelievable chance she took.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had escaped from concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere, or ghettos. I don't know. And my mother took them up to Budapest. There was some organization, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] , maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. More with Budapest, more important people, you know, who could-- there were very few people who could do-- escape from Poland. But there were a few. And it was your life was in danger if you took them in. And they couldn't speak Hungarian. And anyway, my mother felt that she has to do good for everybody that comes to her. She was that kind of a self-sacrificing human being. She felt for every human being. Her children came first, but after that, humanity. She was a real humanitarian. And thank God we have learned this from her, and even our children, and I think our grandchildren, know this, that we come from a good family where we care for every human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened when you arrived-- when you arrived two days later to-- to where they were taking you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we hear all this screaming. It sounded like animals screaming, and German.

speaker: interviewer
text: The train stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train stopped. And they pushed us out fast. Everything was fast. And lined us up, I guess five, because that was their magic number.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean five? What do you mean they lined you up five?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, five in every line, in every row, I think. Or maybe-- maybe we were more as we came off the train. And all of a sudden, we see this gorgeous German soldier, officer, with big German shepherds, you know, and yelling and screaming. And we thought that we arrived in an insane asylum. It had no reality factor. And I think that I remember that my mind went also like berserk. This is not real. And I went into another world, immediately into shock.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember this German soldier who you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, his name-- well, later on, not then, but a couple of days later we found out that he was Mengele.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was the most important man there. And he had to say life or death. His right arm meant life, his left was death. We didn't know this until we arrived to the cells. You know, but-- first he took out my older sister Tipi, Chicha, and Isabelle to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: He told them to go to the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did we do that, just with his hand?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, fast, fast, fast. And then the line-- I went with my mother, my little sister. And I guess my brother was with men, so I don't remember what happened to him. But they-- they turned us around when I was with my mother and my little sister. And they turned us around, and they said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. Mengele, because he didn't take out enough people to the right hand side, which we only knew retroactively, you know, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: He turned around to look at you again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, yeah. And he pulled me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And put you to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- to the right hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: You originally went to the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Left, with my mother. And I think that I was put out there, even though I was young, I was busty, and I looked-- the Hungarian women looked healthy. And even I looked healthy. And then when I saw him pull me out, I looked at my mother and my little sister. And I said to Mengele, [GERMAN]. My little sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said she's only 13 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: 13. Let her go anyway, I said. Let her go with my mother. I didn't know they're going to their death. But my mother knew it. I'm sure of that. And that hurts. And I will never get over that, never in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were trying--

speaker: interviewee
text: To walk into your death. Not many people knew that, but my mother knew it from the Polish Jews. And she was a very alert woman who knew politics and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your sister was already on the left with your mother anyway?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. But I still feel that I had something-- I had said the wrong thing. I didn't know then. I thought I'm doing my little sister a favor. Let her go with my mother, she is so attached to her. But then, ever since then, I feel that I killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I made her go with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well it seems to me Mengele put her on the left side.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're so right, but I can't say that. I-- because I said that she is only 13, I blame myself, 50 years now, that I killed my little sister. One's mind, when it goes berserk, how can you tell that person how I should feel?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remained with this feeling up until today. And I told my doctor that it doesn't look likely that I am going to get over this. And it's been-- actually crippled me all these 50 years. There were other things too, but this is the major theme of my illness.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your illness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I became a depressive, and seriously depressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not depressed before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No, no, no. I was-- you know, kind of very active child, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you saw your mother on the left and your sister on the left. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then they were screaming and yelling. They took us into this place where they undressed us, and they put a rag on us, and they take our hair off.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- what do you mean they take your-- they shaved?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- they shaved our head. And we looked at each other, and we didn't recognize each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was doing the shaving of the hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: There may have been Polish or Russian or some kind of people who already were there in Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewer
text: Prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Prisoners who did that. But our minds were totally gone. And we said this is an insane asylum. And we hardly recognized each other. And you didn't know where you were. And you couldn't think that this is, really in a way, your death, the end of your life. Even though I'm alive today, I think my life somehow ended at that moment, because I've never been normal since then. I had been so affected.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean you've been depressed since then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Depressed, going from one doctor to another for at least 40 years or 42 years, yes. And it's my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what happened though after they shaved your hair. Do they shave anything else, or just your heads?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they put us in this large, what they call Lager C.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is a camp, lager is camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they had these big rooms where there were only like wooden boxes. And each wooden box had to hold 12 of us, like we were into each other like-- not animals, because animals have some--

speaker: interviewer
text: Space.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Some way to move. We didn't. We just were fitted kind of into each other, 12 people in every one of those. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rows--

speaker: interviewee
text: --think they had a thousand people in every lager. And there were thousands of-- and there were I don't know how many in a lager.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your sisters all, and you, were you all together? Did you remain together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We remained together. That was definitely so because we had to. We made an agreement that we're going to try to stay together. Till the bitter end we did stay together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where your mother and your baby sister went?

speaker: interviewee
text: All that time. And we got there, these block altestes, who worked with the Germans, screamed, unheard of voices, you know, like very deep and very loud, if you're not going to do this or that, you going to go up. Do you see that smoke? That's where your mother is. And you're going to go up in that smoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what they were talking about when they said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: How could you believe that? You-- I mean, you're coming from-- not just two months ago, I was in my home. And you-- you just don't know who you are, don't know where you are. And you know, a hopelessness and some kind of almost madness. Your mind becomes, you know, like a veil over your mind, you know? And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and we remained there for quite a few months. Went through selections, which was the biggest farce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me an idea what a day was like. You woke up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you had to get up early  in the morning and line up in fives.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's early? What do you mean when you say early?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably 6 o'clock. I don't know. And each block or each you know, cell or something, had a thousand people in it. And you had to line up in five. Now, we were five sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rows of five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rows of five, up onto a thousand. And you stood there for hours, maybe-- maybe five, six hours or more. I don't remember. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just stood there, did nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did nothing. And the sun and the horrible climate burning you down. And then a German woman, Grese, came with a dog for selection.

speaker: interviewer
text: A German Shepherd?

speaker: interviewee
text: A German Shepherd. Grese, of course, was murdered, you know, hung after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Grese?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grese was the woman equivalent of Dr. Mengele. You know, she was a big shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Grese was her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the way she was called. We don't know any other name, Grese.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were only women in your-- in your-- in your camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: In my camp, only women.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you'd stand in the sun. And then she came. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she looks through every row and counts every people, that nobody is missing. Well, where could we go? This was called selection. And if somebody didn't look so good, she pulled them out. And I guess they got them together from the whole camp, and they took them to the crematorium. That we learned later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they didn't look so good, you mean they didn't look healthy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Healthy or strong. And my sisters were terribly worried about me because I was so little. And they used to make my cheeks a little redder. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went like this, like this. You know, and they kind of pushed me up. I should look taller. You know? And they were terribly worried about me. That's why I think my little sister would have never survived, because I hardly survived, and she was five years younger than I was. And I still feel that that I killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How could the mind be so when the Germans did everything? We didn't do anything. We tried to be a little bit human, even when we were standing at the zahlappell. Zahlappell is when they counted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Counted you, yeah. Now, from then on, we figured it out that we should hide from every zahlappell, the five of us. And we somehow managed to do this for months.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you possibly manage that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, when we heard that they're coming, we-- went-- it was a very big place. We'd go into the washroom, or we'd hide. We were just running like maniacs because we didn't want to see one of us taken away or two of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- I asked you about the day. Did you have jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister Chicha--

speaker: interviewer
text: Berta?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berta. Actually, Isabelle got the job in a place called [GERMAN]--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, in our block, where they selected all the clothes that we brought in. And because of that, she got one piece of bread-- which wasn't bread, I think. It was sawdust. But that was more than not having it. And then she got some kind of little typhoid, and we had to take care of her. And she pushed Chicha into her job. And Chicha came home one night with a knife. And that was like having everything. You didn't have to break your bread. You could, for five people, cut very thin pieces and-- and, you know, divide it equally.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food, other than the bread? Did you have food?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one soup, once a day, maybe in the afternoon. And it was like-- how do you say, a big sty, what they give for pigs? You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Slop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Slop. It looked like slop.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who gave it out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- I don't know whether they were prisoners, or Germans came with a truck or something. They were always screaming about everything, you know? Like we should stay here, we should stay there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well I didn't think that I would remember all this much. So you have helped me to remember all this. I don't want to remember it. It still hurts. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every moment was a fear from the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Crematorium is where they burnt the human bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- yeah, they first gassed them. Sometimes I heard they didn't even die.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what? Our tape is up. And we're going to come back and talk to you again right after this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
